risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Subjects were adult cigarette smokers randomly switched to a tobacco-heating cigarette ... snus ... or an ultra-low machine yield tobacco-burning cigarette ..." and "The intent-to-treat sample was used for week 0 analyses and included all randomized subjects in the groups to which they were assigned..." Comment: The paper states that subjects were randomized/randomly switched, but it does not describe how the allocation sequence was generated (e.g. computer-generated list, random number table, block randomization, stratification). 'Randomized' alone without additional methodological detail is insufficient to confirm a truly random sequence, and there is no explicit evidence of a non-random method either.

Summary: The article states that subjects were "randomly switched" to product groups and reports randomized sample sizes, but it does not describe any allocation concealment mechanism (e.g., central randomization, pharmacy-controlled allocation, or sequentially numbered opaque sealed envelopes). Comment: Randomization alone addresses sequence generation, not whether the person enrolling participants could foresee the upcoming assignment; with no explicit concealment details, this domain is unclear.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Subjects were adult cigarette smokers randomly switched to a tobacco-heating cigarette (Eclipse, non-menthol or menthol variety depending on subject preference), snus (Camel SNUS, subject choice of Frost, Spice, and Mellow varieties) or an ultra-low machine yield tobacco-burning cigarette..." Comment: The interventions were clearly different, so participants and study personnel delivering the products would have known group assignment; no blinding procedure is described. Because several study outcomes in this study context (e.g. smoking cessation/adherence-related behavior and adverse events) are plausibly influenced by knowledge of allocation, lack of blinding could affect outcomes. Therefore performance bias is judged high overall.

Quote: "Samples for 24-h urine, spot urine and fasting blood were collected and analyzed for biomarkers of exposure..." and "Urine samples were then shipped in batch to the appropriate analytical laboratory for analysis." and "Blood samples were collected... stored until the end of the study, and then shipped to the appropriate analytical laboratory for analysis." Comment: The outcomes assessed in this report were laboratory/analyzer-based biomarkers (urine and blood assays, CO-oximeter measures, GC/LC-MS analyses) and instrument-based mouth-level exposure measurements, so the assessors were primarily lab/imaging system or clinical staff recording standardized physical measurements rather than subjective raters. The article does not explicitly state that laboratory personnel were blinded to allocation, but all reported outcomes in this paper are objective biomarker or device-based measures that are unlikely to be influenced by knowledge of allocation. No patient-reported or clinician-judged subjective outcomes were reported here, so under the worst-outcome rule this domain is Low risk.

Quote: "The intent-to-treat sample was used for week 0 analyses and included all randomized subjects... Sample sizes for the intent-to-treat sample were: 44 switched to tobacco-heating cigarettes, 43 switched to snus and 44 switched to ultra-low machine yield tobacco-burning cigarettes... The per-protocol sample was used for change-from-baseline analyses and product group comparisons at weeks 12 and 24, and was defined by mean cumulative compliance greater than 50%... Sample sizes for the per-protocol sample were: 33 switched to tobacco-heating cigarettes, 20 switched to snus and 35 switched to ultra-low machine yield tobacco-burning cigarettes... Dropouts were not replaced and missing data were not imputed." Comment: For the analysed week 12/24 outcomes, the paper reports that analyses were restricted to a per-protocol subset rather than all randomized participants. Compared with randomized numbers, analysed numbers were 33/44 (25.0% loss/exclusion) in the tobacco-heating arm, 20/43 (53.5%) in the snus arm, and 35/44 (20.5%) in the ultra-low-yield cigarette arm. This is substantial overall attrition/exclusion with large between-arm imbalance (>10 percentage points, especially the snus arm), and the exclusions were based on post-randomization compliance, materially deviating from random assignment. No imputation was used. These reported numbers constitute positive evidence that incomplete outcome data could bias the outcome estimates.
